finding: A valid lifecycle interpretation remains replayable unless final acceptance is bound to a unique opaque acceptance identifier and a verifier-maintained seen-set. Component validity, navigation identity, and lifecycle epoch identity do not by themselves prove one-time use.

primary_sources.title: RFC 9449: OAuth 2.0 Demonstrating Proof of Possession
primary_sources.relevance: Uses a unique proof identifier and replay detection, and states that a valid proof alone is insufficient for an access-control decision. The artifact transfers only this replay-resistance pattern, not the OAuth protocol.

design_implication: Place a one-time acceptance gate after lifecycle composition. Require a fresh opaque acceptance_id, reject identifiers already present in the verifier-maintained seen-set, and retain only opaque identifiers plus reason codes.

verification.environment: isolated Node runtime using byte-identical pre-commit files

uncertainty: The implementation uses an in-memory caller-supplied seen-set contract. Persistence, bounded retention, concurrency, crash consistency, and multi-tab coordination are not implemented.
falsification_route: Demonstrate that replaying the same previously accepted lifecycle interpretation cannot cause duplicate state transition, counting, or evidence acceptance.
Produce a fresh valid acceptance identifier that the gate incorrectly rejects.
Show that a duplicate identifier can be accepted under concurrent verifier updates.
Implement persistent multi-tab storage and compare its observed behavior with this pure-function contract.
